design_philosophy.core_loop: Infiltrate castle levels, defeat bosses to acquire color-matched swords, solve environmental puzzles to determine correct level/jewel order, return jewels to altar in correct sequence
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Uncertainty about optimal progression path; dual-character control complexity in single-player mode creates execution pressure
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Falconer adventurer and companion falcon infiltrating enemy castles to retrieve legendary artifacts and restore order after apocalyptic collapse
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Refuses to hold player's hand on level order or jewel placement; requires note-taking and lateral thinking. Asymmetrical co-op design (one player controls both characters solo) creates fundamentally different difficulty experiences. Two distinct narrative identities (post-apocalyptic vs. Gothic 19th century Balkans) between regions suggest designer ambition beyond typical platformer scope.

difficulty.lives_system: Health bar system with extensions that grant additional hit capacity
difficulty.continues_system: Password system allows mid-game saves; US version expanded with three separate quest progression paths
difficulty.checkpointing: No within-level checkpoints; password-based mid-game saves only
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: cooperative mode significantly reduces difficulty vs. single-player dual-character control. Level order is player-chosen (first 7 levels), allowing difficulty customization through sequence selection. Multi-quest structure (US version) allows players to tackle incrementally harder playthroughs.

progression.upgrade_system: Sword acquisition system: each boss defeated yields a new sword with unique color; only the color-matched sword deals optimal damage to subsequent bosses. Swords are not inherently 'stronger' but situationally superior. Health extensions unlock through hidden collectibles.
progression.unlock_mechanism: First 7 castle levels freely selectable in any order; final 'House of Ruth' level locked until all seven defeated. US version: Special stage auto-triggers after main game; Final stage requires post-credits reset. Passwords gate access to additional quests.
replayability_hooks: Correct level sequence is hidden (clue scrolls scattered throughout game)
Jewel placement order at endgame is also hidden (requires clue collection)
US version's three separate quest tiers with escalating difficulty
Cooperative vs. single-player mode offers fundamentally different challenges
Regional differences create incentive to replay (Japanese sound test, different clue wording)

world_building_techniques: Divergent regional narratives: post-apocalyptic (US) vs. Gothic supernatural (Japan) create two complete thematic frameworks
Hidden clue scrolls embedded in level design force environmental reading
Boss encounters double as narrative progression (each defeated duke represents power structure dismantling)
Final puzzle (jewel sequence) is lore-gated: incorrect arrangement triggers loss; correct arrangement validates player comprehension
Japanese ending reveals dark twist: Baphomet statue awakening despite victory, suggesting hollow triumph
Title character (Orin/Bond) paired with falcon companion establishes relationship-driven narrative core

boss_design.philosophy: Each of eight dukes represents a unique threat requiring specific counter-strategy. Boss vulnerability tied directly to progression system: color-matched sword grants significant damage advantage. Design emphasizes pattern recognition and resource management (sword selection) as much as execution.
notable_bosses: Egypt (Red Jewel) - Defeated with Red Sword
Arabia (White Jewel) - Defeated with White Sword (US version: acquired late, forcing strategic reordering)
House of Ruth - Final stage boss with demon resurrection plot (Japanese version narrative focus)

faction_indicators: The Eight Dukes: power-hungry nobility stealing jewels (antagonistic faction)
College of Arms/MI5 (Japanese lore): institutional order attempting to prevent supernatural resurrection
Ruth Grandier's cult: demonic worship organization claiming Lucifer lineage
The Great King: benevolent monarchy figure imprisoned in wastelands
danger_signals: Nuclear mutants encountered as regular enemies signal post-apocalyptic contamination
Missing heads and eviscerated bodies (Japanese version backstory) establish cult's brutality
Baphomet statue awakening despite player victory implies hidden threat beyond surface narrative
Jewel misplacement at endgame triggers failure state: wrong order = wrong master awakens
Dukes' hoarding of artifacts that 'concealed demonic secrets' suggests widespread danger if improperly handled
environmental_storytelling: Eight distinct castle locations (Spain, Egypt, Arabia, Italy, India, Africa, Germany) establish geopolitical scope
Nuclear explosion centers where jewels formed (post-apocalyptic narrative grounding)
Ruins of British Museum excavation (Japanese version) set Gothic archaeological frame
Hidden door leading to Baphomet statue and stolen treasures reveals secret power structure
Altar of Peace location functions as sanctuary/restoration point (redemption geography)
